result:
The old cherry tree in the backyard doesn't bloom for anyone. It simply does, a stubborn burst of pink against the grey April drizzle. This morning, a single branch has dipped so low it brushes my study window, its blossoms blurring into watercolor streaks on the glass. I stopped typing to watch a sparrow land on a thin twig, setting off a cascade of droplets. For a moment, the tiny tremor seemed monumental, a whole universe shaking in that wet, feathered heartbeat.

We spend so much time building—careers, arguments, walls of reason—and yet the world’s most profound statements are often these quiet, unearned offerings. A tree that remembers nothing, that struggles against frost and pest, still decides to be beautiful. There’s a reckless generosity in that. It doesn’t ask for my attention, nor does it apologize when the petals fall, littering the lawn like pink confetti from a party I wasn’t invited to.

Later, I will sweep the petals into a pile. But for now, I sit still, letting the wet beauty hold my gaze. The screen saver flickers on, a forgotten slide of digital clouds. I have nothing to add. The tree has said enough.
